sentences.text: No.
The brothel was outside the Kaunas Ghetto.
The brothel was in the fire street, which in Lithuanian, that calls the [LITHUANIAN] street, fire street in Kupiskis [LITHUANIAN], Kupiskis building, it calls, a block of flats.
And we were doing quite a lot of business with these girls because they demanded from us-- actually, not demanded-- they asked us to bring them some watches, shoes, clothing, anything.
And we used to bring them and got some money for it.
And we used to buy food from there, because they used to arrange people to come to the building, and we used to buy.
And the Germans used to take us back.
Every 3:00 in the afternoon, he used to take us back to the ghetto by truck.
And he used to drive in the truck into the ghetto, which he wasn't allowed to do that.
But as Lithuanian police, we're at the gate.
So he used to shout it out to the Lithuanian police to open the gate.
And the Lithuanian police had respect for a German.
So they used to open the gate and he is to drive in the truck and turn around.
It was a little place to turn around the truck.
And by turning-- when he used to turn the truck, we used to throw out the parcels, whatever we had, from the truck.
And he used to drive out.
And that was the procedure every day, the same.
And one day was a funny story.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] The river wasn't far from that place, a couple hundred meters.
And the Lithuanian fishermen brought a pike.
It was about 10, 12 kilo-size pike.
You know what the pike is.
It's a longer fish.
And we didn't have time really to pick the pike.
But that plumber of ours said, I'll take the pike up in a box upstairs in the room.
And I'll let water in, and tomorrow morning we will take the fish out.
And when we arrived in the ghetto, we told all our-- all the families wives that tomorrow we're going to have fish.
We're going to have fish.
And everybody's talking about that pike of fish.
And then we arrived the next day, everybody was running to the back to see how is the fish doing?
And the fish was already in the ghetto, taken by that plumber.
He tied a little rope around his neck, and behind his coat he got the fish in-- into the ghetto.
And he said, you all stupid.
You think that I'll leave the fish for you?
My wife has already cooked that fish.
This was a nice story for us.
We were laughing, all of us there.
Anyway, it carried on like this for six weeks.
We were in that place for six weeks.
We had wonderful six weeks doing very little jobs, which other people used to be driven out on quite terrible jobs, digging trenches and so on.
And when the gebiz kommissar stopped us going there, I-- one day I took off my stars and I went myself from the ghetto.
I went out with the other group of people to work.
And when I reached a certain corner of the street in town, I went out onto the corner.
I went out on the pavement.
And I went in town, and nobody recognized me that I'm Jewish.
And I went to the German, and he was surprised seeing me.
He says, [GERMAN].
How did you come here?
I said, well, I said, that's a different story.
Let's talk.
And I brought him an electric kettle present that he should see-- see what he can do if he can get the job back.
But unfortunately, he didn't come back to us.
He didn't-- he was afraid.
He says, I'm afraid to stick out my neck.
And that was the end of the job from there.
Motel?
And we had to--
I'll tell you.
My one brother-- my two brothers were in Africa, one in Rhodesia and one in Johannesburg, South Africa in Krugersdorp.
The other brother we left-- one brother we left in Russia.
He remained in Russia, which is a little story I'll tell you later.
And my sisters were all in the shtetl.
My three sisters were in the shtetl.
And when they first-- what they did, the German-- the Lithuanian, actually, what they did is they collected the strongest men from the shtetl, the biggest men, the strongest one.
And they took him near the church, which I-- after the war, I saw where the place was.
And they shot him.
There were about 12 of them.
And they were then dragged down near the river, and they were buried there.
My father was-- my father died in 1935.
When I was in the army, my father died.
He died of-- he was ill for a while, and he was died.
Yeah.
She started going to labor early in the morning.
And when I was in the army, in the Lithuanian Army, I knew one professor, by the name Professor Zacharin.
He was a gynecologist.
And I went to him.
I knew that he's in ghetto.
I knew where it is.
We already kept an eye on this.
And I went to him, and he says, it'll cost you 1 and 1/2 kilograms of bread for me to come to take the child.
I said, quite all right.
Come.
So I brought him to our place.
And we warmed up water.
And he took out the child, and he was perfect.
And the child grew up until he was 18 months.
A little boy.
And what was his name?
Henech.
He was after my father's name.
My father was Henech.
So we gave him a name after my father.
And I didn't let him have the bris milah in ghetto, because I said to Ida, if we'll survive, we'll make him a bris, but not now.
Leave it.
So he had no bris.
What was the reason for that?
Why didn't you want to--
The reason for that was, I had in my mind I'm going to take him out from the ghetto to give it to Lithuanian people to help through the time.
So I eventually find a farmer.
Shall I come to that a little bit later?
But that was when the baby was born in the ghetto.
I'll come to that later a little bit.
But then we had to register ourselves to work on the airport.
So being an electrician, we were 22 Jewish electricians.
And we did all the electrical installations for the Germans.
They build barracks.
Then they built a new airport.
And we did the electrical installation in the new airport, all the Yiddish boys.
And my foreman on the airport was a Christian guy, which I was quite friendly with him.
And he used to say to me, take off your star.
And we're going to have lunch at the German unit, at the German unit with the Germans army.
And there were German soldiers amongst them, and I used to go in.
Nobody knew that I'm Jewish or not.
I had a bit of chutzpah as to go for another helping-- take the tray and go for another helping.
And that lasted for three weeks, and then we were transferred to another unit, which I couldn't go there anymore.
And the Jewish people in ghetto were never starved of starvation if they wouldn't be shot, because everybody find a way how to get through with a little bit of product.
We used to take in-- one day they used to take away the-- the food from us, and the next day we carry again.
It was nonstop.
I beg your pardon?
Certain times they used to give us a brick, kicking in our-- in our ass-- kicking us and go.
But on certain, they were quite harsh if they would find money or something like this, and we'll come back to money a little bit later, what happened to me.
And we worked on the airport.
On the airport used to come also Lithuanian farmers from the area, from around us.
They used to bring some cement, bricks, this that.
So we got in touch with the Lithuanian.
We start talking to them-- I start talking to them.
If one day we escape from the ghetto, if they will let us-- if they will help us a little bit.
Of course, there are some of them yes, some no.
There are some people good ones, some bad ones.
So some of them, like a farmer said, you bring out your little boy, my wife-- I spoke to her-- she'll take him.
And those days, the Germans used to deport kids from Russia.
On the railway station, they used to sell them at [?
Tembo Beach ?].
So I took talked in that farmer that his wife bought this on the railway station for [?
Tembo ?].
So it was fine.
And one day, being on the airport, I saw trucks-- railway trucks-- barbed with barbed wire, doors and windows with barbed wire.
And they stopped on the airport.
There must have been about 100 trucks, and that was in the middle of the day, something about 3:00 in the afternoon.
And I saw it that-- and I thought that's definitely for us.
Because people were talking already that they're going to deport from the Jewish-- Jewish to Estonia, something like this.
So what I did is I took off my stars, and I escaped from work, and I went down-- to come to the ghetto, I had to come across two rivers by the name the Nemen and the Vilia.
So I went down and I find fishing boats.
They were not locked.
So I jumped into fishing boat.
I sailed across the one river, and I walked a little island, the same that river.
And I came to the ghetto.
And I said, Ida-- my wife was Ida-- [GERMAN] Ida, I'm sure that the railway trucks, which I saw it now with barbed wire, must be for us.
Just let's take out our little boy, and we already had to little arrangements with the police-- Jewish policeman.
He will help us to take out the little boy.
And when that same afternoon, when the mass of people came to the ghetto, that policeman, I had my wife-- my little boy I had under a raincoat, holding him under a raincoat with a sleeping tablet.
He was asleep.
Then he pushed me through the gate.

And I walked across the two bridges, which I had to cross to go to the farm, having my little boy on my shoulders-- he was blonde-- and singing Lithuanian songs past the police, the police and military Germans, and so on.
And they did, they just smiled at me.
They just laughed at me that I'm singing to my little boy.
Well, obviously, she went through a very, very hard time, and the same as us.
When we were freed by the Russians, we walked around in many, many places to look.
Maybe there's a mistake or so on.
But we found out that he was killed by the Lithuanian where he was kept, but this is still to come.

I didn't mention-- I didn't mention when I started off in Russia, walked into Lithuania-- I didn't mention that after a couple of months, the Russians arrested the rich people from Lithuania, and they deported them to Siberia.
They kept them on the railway station.
They kept them three days without water and food.
We were in that area.
We could-- we know what's happening.
Well, after the war, some of them survived but most of them not.
When?
Yeah.
Yeah.
I will carry on with this.
When I took out my little boy that night, the ghetto was surrounded by hundreds of police and so on.
And that night they took out about 10,000 people from the ghetto.
My wife managed to survive because we had a little pantry in the [GERMAN].
We had a little pantry there with firewood.
So she climbed in between the firewood, and she covered herself with firewood, with logs.
And when they came in to search, they didn't find anybody.
They walked out.
They searched each room where-- to take the people.
And two days later, she came on the airport with a mate of mine, came to the-- we made arrangements where I will meet here.
And she came, and I took her away to the-- I took her on the farmland.
I made arrangements with the farmer.
He was quite happy to have her.
I got a job somewhere else.
That was me-- for me to get a job was easy, because I looked-- I could stay in the field and work and people walk up to me and they don't know who they're talking to.
On the farms, [GERMAN].
We're farming anything, cutting grass, feeding pigs, feeding cattle, everything.
I stayed in a stable in the hay.
And we stayed there for quite a while until four months before the war end.
I went into the-- I went to the farm, and I used to visit them.
And the farm told me bad news that I'll have to take away my little boy from the farm, because the two brothers-- farmers-- had a fight between themselves.
And one said to the other, get rid of that little Jew boy, otherwise I'm going to report you to the police.
And the next time when I came, the farmer told me, look here, you'll have to take away little boy.
What could I do?
I then went to look for a place for my little boy.
He was quite a good guy.
He gave me a letter to a mate of his, which it was 25 kilometers away, to a forester.
And the forester-- and I knew that area, and what was 25 kilometers to me?
Nothing those days.
So I went to the forester with that letter, hoping that they're going to take my little boy.
But when I arrived, why, the farmer-- the forester wasn't there.
He was somewhere in the forest, and his wife was there.
And his wife was there.
And she read that letter, and she start screaming and shouting at me.
Get out of here, you [GERMAN], you Jew, Judas.
And she start screaming, and she kicked me out from the house just like this.
What could I do?
I just walked back.
I started to walk back.
And on a certain bend in the forest, the road was curving.
Three Lithuanian policemen with machine gun on their shoulders come towards me, and I went up to them.
I wore a Lithuanian cap, and I passed by.
I didn't get the feeling to run.
I just passed by them, and I greeted them.
I took off my cap, and they saluted me, and they passed by.
And they were looking for me.
And since then, I came back, and the Lithuanian used to say where there are small snakes, there must be big snakes also.
They start looking for me.
And the farmers, wherever I used to go in, they were afraid to keep me because they said the police are searching terrible.
Anyway, I went to Ida's.
I went-- from there, I went still to my farmer, and that was end of '43.
My little boy, I find for him-- yeah.
Coming back, I had to go somewhere else to look for-- eventually, I find a place.
And my little boy, I find for him a decent place, and three weeks later, they surrounded the area.
And they find my little boy, and they find next boy a farmer, a little Yiddish [GERMAN], and they arrested him and shot him.
Two and a half.
He was two and a half.
Anyway, we knew that the little boy was not alive anymore.
And I went to Ida's farm.
And we talked in Ida's farm.
Yeah.
I'll come to that.
There's so much to talk about.
Yeah, I will [INAUDIBLE] now.
I went back to the farm.
That was the end of '43.
And a couple of months earlier, by the farmer where I stayed, one day came a Russian prisoner of war.
And he was also accepted by that farmer to work for them.
They had quite a big job to do and accepted him.
The farmer itself, he was a sickly person.
He was somewhat cripple.
And his wife was still quite a youngish woman.
So the Russian prisoner of war started to have a good life with that-- with his wife.
And Christmastime they had a party.
I wasn't attending.
I was in the hay.
I was afraid to go out, but they were all there.
And the farmer's wife and the Russian prisoner of war decided that they're going to kill me.
They're going to kill me.
Why?
Because they always used to say that Jewish people have a lot of money on them.
And I think good boots, and I had a leather coat, and I had good clothing, and he was my size.
And Christmas Eve after the party, he came in to me, and he took all my clothes-- all my clothes.
Usually, we used to sleep in the house, but that Christmas Eve we weren't sleeping there.
I was out.
He took all my clothes, and he went.
And in my trousers, I had about 5,000 or 6,000 rubles, something like this.
So having money, he went into farmer, and he bought liquor, and he got drunk.
He went in to fight with the farmer.
And the farmer phoned the police.
And the police came out, and they arrested him.
They arrested him.
And apparently, being under arrest, he told the police that there and there and there in that farm is a Jew, like me.
And on the 5th of January in 1944, 1:00 in the morning, the farm place where I stayed was surrounded by police-- by seven policeman.
And they started to shout loud, everybody out of the house, otherwise we put the house on fire.
So obviously, we had to get out.
And I went out.
They threw me on the ground, and they smashed me up.
But I was quite strong to withhold all that fight-- the [INAUDIBLE] they gave me.
And they put me against the wall of the stable.
And seven of them, with the instruct from the officer in charge to shoot me.
And I was standing against the wall, and that was the end of me, I thought myself.
But then he said, stop.
Don't shoot him.
We better hang him.
We better hang him.
So they asked the farmer to take out a rope.
He threw a rope on the tree and made a noose, and put me on a little box and the noose on my throat-- my head.
And I thought that that's the end-- a couple of minutes and that's the end for me.
But then he said stop, stop.
We no time.
We'll take him alive.
We'll take him alive, because he knows all the secrets from this area.
So by knowing all the secrets from this area, they will take me alive.
And they will find out all this what they want to find out.
So they asked the farmer for a sled with two horses.
And we went into it, seven around me and we go.
It was already about 3:00 in the morning.
And we went for a couple of kilometers.
And the officer in charge stopped us again, and he says he suspect that this and this farmer must have again-- must have again Jewish.
Let's search that farm.
And the five of them went in to search the farm.
And two of them took me into a house-- into the farmer's house, put the side board across.
And I was in the corner, and they stood with the guns in front of me, the two of them.
And I then decided that I'm going to escape from these two policemen.
So I started to beg them that I want to wee.
And one shouted-- shout at me to do it in your trousers, you this and this, and so and so.
But the other one was a little softer.
He says, let's take him out.
If he'll try to escape, we'll shoot him down like a dog.
So they told me to have my hands behind, and they will open the door for me.
And as they opened the door, I jumped fast and corner, corner, corner of the house.
And I started to run between the fruit trees, and opened fire.
And the five joined in, and I escaped.
They didn't get me.
The bullets were just running past me like you can't believe it.
And I was running zigzag and so on.
And they didn't get me, and I escaped.
At this time, where was Ida?
Ida was in the same farm-- the same farm where she was before.
I went to her farm where Ida was.
It was about 6:00 in the morning already.
I did that night about 45 kilometers running away from them and coming back to Ida's place.
And then we went in.
Nobody knew what's happened to me.
And Ida says, Motel, we heard so much shooting outside.
What happened?
Was it on you?
I said, Ida, that was me.
They shot at me.
But they didn't get me, and I'm here.
I'm here to ask-- let's ask the farmer if we'll dig out a hole somewhere for us to go down under the wardrobe in a bedroom, say like this room, a bedroom, and the main bedroom of the farmer.
So we talked him into cut-- to cut the [GERMAN] in the wardrobe and in the floor.
And we digged out a 6 x 3 x 3 hole, and we laid there 100 days. 100 days.
100 days.
Yeah.
We had a bucket.
And that was our dining room table with a lid.
And that was our toilet.
That was everything.
And everything, the farmer's wife and the family used to take out the bucket.
[INAUDIBLE] and bring it back, the bucket.
